---
title: "UIAO Modernization Master Plan — Phase 1 Architecture Document"
subtitle: "Plane model, interfaces, signal taxonomy, and control bindings"
doc-type: master-plan
phase: "Phase 1 — Architecture"
audience: "CIO · CISO · ISSO · Authorizing Official (AO) · 3PAO · Enterprise Architects"
canon-source: "src/uiao/canon/modernization-registry.yaml"
derived-from: "phase-0-planning.qmd"
upstream:
  - "phase-0-planning.qmd §2 Modernization Scope"
  - "phase-0-planning.qmd §4 FedRAMP Boundary Gap"
  - "phase-0-planning.qmd §5 Objectives & Outcomes"
aspirational: true
date: 2026-04-26
date-format: "MMMM D, YYYY"
---

# UIAO Modernization Master Plan — Phase 1 Architecture Document {.unnumbered}

::: {.callout-note}
## Document Purpose
Phase 1 formalizes the UIAO architecture into a normative reference model.
It defines each plane's responsibility surface, the inter-plane interface
contracts, the canonical signal taxonomy, the control binding model, and
the conformance criteria a deployment must satisfy. This document
consumes the Phase 0 Planning Document (Sections 2, 4, 5) as normative
input and produces the architecture baseline that the Boundary
Modernization Appendix, Compliance & ATO Alignment, and 90-Day Execution
Plan derive from.
:::

---

## 1. Architectural Principles

The UIAO architecture is governed by seven design principles. Every
plane, interface, and signal in this document conforms to all seven; any
deviation is an explicit, attributed exception recorded in an ADR.

| # | Principle | Architectural Consequence |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Deterministic State** | Every governed entity has exactly one canonical state at any point in time; conflicts are resolved by canonical-source authority, not by last-writer-wins |
| 2 | **Schema Fixity** | Schemas are immutable once canonized; values are flexible within governed enumerations |
| 3 | **Provenance Traceability** | Every state change carries an attributed source — human, automation, or governance engine — and is reconstructable from the evidence chain |
| 4 | **Drift Resistance** | Drift is detected, classified (schema, value, hierarchy, orphan, phantom), and remediated under governed workflow |
| 5 | **Boundary Enforcement** | No artifact, signal, or policy crosses the authorization boundary without an attributed enforcement decision |
| 6 | **Two-Brain Execution** | Governance logic and execution logic are physically separated; the governance brain decides, the execution brain acts |
| 7 | **Tenant Agnosticism** | Architecture is portable across any conforming tenant; environment-specific identifiers are injected at deployment time |

These principles are normative. A conforming Phase 2 design that violates
one of them is not a UIAO deployment; it is a different architecture.

---

## 2. The Plane Model

UIAO is composed of ten planes. Each plane is a first-class architectural
unit with a defined responsibility, a signal surface it produces and
consumes, an interface contract with adjacent planes, and a set of
control bindings.

### 2.1 Plane Catalog

| Plane | Responsibility | Produces | Consumes |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Identity** | Canonical principal state, role, group, AU, lifecycle | Identity assertion, role binding, lifecycle event | Authoritative HR feed, directory state |
| **Device** | Device posture, management state, attestation | Posture report, compliance state, attestation token | Identity binding, policy directive |
| **Server** | Server identity, workload identity, host posture | Workload identity, host telemetry, server OrgPath | Identity binding, policy directive |
| **Routing** | WAN, SD-WAN, SASE path selection and egress | Route state, path identity, regional tag | Policy directive, identity assertion |
| **Security** | Access decision, Zero Trust enforcement, containment | Access decision, risk signal, response action | Identity, device, routing, policy signals |
| **Evidence** | Provenance graph, signal capture, OSCAL artifacts | Provenance record, OSCAL component, SAR/SSP fragment | All plane signals |
| **Compliance** | Control catalog, KSI mapping, POA&M, assessment | Control state, KSI outcome, POA&M record | Evidence records, policy state |
| **Drift Detection** | Cross-plane drift classification and remediation | Drift record, remediation provenance | All plane signals |
| **Continuous ATO** | Authorization posture, monitoring cadence, AO surface | Authorization state, re-assessment trigger | Compliance state, evidence freshness |
| **Boundary Modernization** | Authorization-boundary signal continuity | Boundary crossing record, signal-continuity assertion | Identity, location, session, telemetry, media, policy signals |

### 2.2 Plane Layering

The planes are not peers; they form a four-tier layering that constrains
which plane may consume which signal.

| Tier | Planes | Role |
|---|---|---|
| **Substrate Tier** | Identity, Device, Server, Routing | Produce primary state about principals, endpoints, workloads, and paths |
| **Enforcement Tier** | Security, Boundary Modernization | Convert substrate signals into access decisions and boundary continuity |
| **Assurance Tier** | Evidence, Drift Detection | Capture provenance, detect divergence, generate audit-ready artifacts |
| **Authorization Tier** | Compliance, Continuous ATO | Map assurance to control catalogs, sustain ongoing authorization posture |

A plane in a higher tier may consume signals from any lower tier; a plane
may not consume signals from a higher tier. This rule prevents
authorization logic from leaking into substrate state and is enforced
architecturally, not procedurally.

---

## 3. Inter-Plane Interfaces

Every plane exposes a typed interface to adjacent planes. Interfaces are
declarative contracts, not transport bindings; the transport (event bus,
API, scheduled pull) is a deployment choice that does not affect
conformance.

### 3.1 Interface Contract Shape

Each interface is described by five elements:

| Element | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Producer** | The plane that asserts the signal |
| **Consumer** | The plane that observes or acts on the signal |
| **Schema** | Fixed schema for the signal payload (governed by Principle 2) |
| **Provenance** | Required attribution and timestamping fields |
| **Cadence** | Event-driven, periodic, or on-demand semantics |

### 3.2 Canonical Interface Set

| Interface | Producer → Consumer | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **identity-assertion** | Identity → Security, Routing, Boundary Mod | Carries authenticated principal and role context |
| **posture-report** | Device, Server → Security | Conveys compliance and attestation state |
| **path-state** | Routing → Security, Boundary Mod | Reports current WAN/SASE path and regional tag |
| **access-decision** | Security → Evidence, Drift Detection | Records every enforcement decision with attribution |
| **boundary-crossing** | Boundary Mod → Evidence, Compliance | Asserts signal continuity (or its absence) at each boundary hop |
| **provenance-record** | Evidence → Compliance, Continuous ATO | Immutable record consumable by audit and assessment |
| **drift-record** | Drift Detection → Compliance, Continuous ATO | Classified divergence with remediation status |
| **control-state** | Compliance → Continuous ATO | Per-control posture used by AO decision support |
| **authorization-state** | Continuous ATO → (operator surface) | The current ongoing authorization posture |

### 3.3 Forbidden Interfaces

Three interface patterns are explicitly disallowed:

1. **Substrate ↔ substrate cross-binding.** Identity must not consume
   Device state directly; the Security plane is the only authorized
   consumer of joined substrate state. This preserves single-source
   authority.
2. **Authorization → substrate writeback.** Compliance and Continuous
   ATO may not write to substrate planes; they consume only.
3. **Evidence bypass.** No interface may carry an enforcement decision
   that does not also produce a provenance-record. Bypassing the
   evidence plane breaks the chain Continuous ATO depends on.

---

## 4. Signal Taxonomy

The signal model formalizes the six classes named in Phase 0 §4.1 plus
two derived classes that the Assurance Tier requires.

### 4.1 Primary Signal Classes (from Phase 0)

| Class | Definition | Producing Plane(s) |
|---|---|---|
| **Identity** | Authenticated principal, role, group, AU, privileged state | Identity |
| **Location** | Originating network context, region, country, segment trust posture | Routing, Boundary Mod |
| **Session** | Continuity of session from initial auth through downstream calls | Identity, Security |
| **Telemetry** | Per-journey observability across planes | All Substrate-Tier planes |
| **Media** | Voice, video, collaboration traffic classification and routing | Routing |
| **Policy** | The set of enforcement decisions applied at each hop, with attribution | Security, Boundary Mod |

### 4.2 Derived Signal Classes (introduced by Phase 1)

| Class | Definition | Producing Plane |
|---|---|---|
| **Provenance** | Attributed record of any state change or enforcement decision | Evidence |
| **Drift** | Classified divergence between expected and observed state | Drift Detection |

### 4.3 Signal Continuity Requirement

Every primary signal class must be carriable end-to-end across all
boundary crossings without attenuation. The Boundary Modernization plane
is the architectural mechanism that satisfies this requirement; its
detailed redesign is the subject of the Boundary Modernization Appendix.

---

## 5. Control Binding Model

Controls bind to planes, not to systems. A FedRAMP Rev 5 control, a
SCuBA baseline policy, or a NIST SP 800-207 tenet attaches to one or
more planes through a typed binding.

### 5.1 Binding Shape

| Field | Purpose |
|---|---|
| **control-id** | Reference into the canonical control library (e.g. AC-2, IA-5, SCuBA EXO-X.Y) |
| **plane** | The plane responsible for satisfying the control |
| **signal** | The signal class on which conformance is evidenced |
| **evidence-pattern** | The provenance-record shape that constitutes proof |
| **assessment-cadence** | How often the binding is re-evaluated for cATO |

### 5.2 Binding Rules

- Every in-scope control has at least one plane binding.
- A control with no observable signal cannot be bound; it is recorded as
  a Compliance-Plane gap and managed through POA&M.
- A control bound to multiple planes requires a defined arbitration
  rule; the Compliance plane is the sole arbiter.

### 5.3 Mandate Coverage Matrix

| Mandate | Primary Bound Planes |
|---|---|
| **EO 14028** | Identity, Device, Security, Evidence |
| **OMB M-22-09 / NIST SP 800-207** | Identity, Device, Routing, Security |
| **CISA SCuBA** | Identity, Security, Evidence, Drift Detection |
| **FedRAMP Rev 5** | All ten planes |
| **Continuous ATO** | Evidence, Compliance, Continuous ATO |

The full per-control crosswalk is produced in the Compliance & ATO
Alignment document; Phase 1 commits only to the binding shape and the
mandate-to-plane coverage.

---

## 6. Reference Architecture View

The reference view is a layered logical topology. It is not a deployment
diagram; specific transports, vendors, and adapters are out of scope for
Phase 1.

```
+------------------------------------------------------------------+
|                     Authorization Tier                           |
|        Compliance  ─────►  Continuous ATO                        |
+------------------------------▲-----------------------------------+
                               | provenance / drift / control-state
+------------------------------┴-----------------------------------+
|                       Assurance Tier                             |
|        Evidence  ◄────────────────────►  Drift Detection         |
+------------------------------▲-----------------------------------+
                               | access-decision / boundary-crossing
+------------------------------┴-----------------------------------+
|                      Enforcement Tier                            |
|   Security  ◄────────────────────►  Boundary Modernization       |
+------------------------------▲-----------------------------------+
                               | identity / posture / path / media
+------------------------------┴-----------------------------------+
|                       Substrate Tier                             |
|   Identity     Device     Server     Routing                     |
+------------------------------------------------------------------+
```

Read top-down, the diagram expresses the architectural rule from §2.2:
signal flows upward, authorization decisions never flow back into the
substrate.

---

## 7. Conformance Criteria

A deployment is Phase 1-conforming when, and only when, all of the
following are demonstrably true:

| # | Criterion | Evidence Surface |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | All ten planes are present and have an attributed owner | Plane registry, owner attestation |
| 2 | Each plane exposes its canonical interfaces with fixed schemas | Schema registry, interface contracts |
| 3 | The four-tier layering rule is enforced — no upward consumption from authorization to substrate | Static topology check, runtime denial logs |
| 4 | Every primary signal class is carriable end-to-end across boundary crossings | Boundary-crossing provenance records |
| 5 | Every in-scope control has at least one plane binding or an explicit POA&M record | Control binding table |
| 6 | Every state change produces a provenance-record consumable by Evidence | Provenance graph completeness scan |
| 7 | Drift detection classifies divergence into the canonical taxonomy | Drift-record sample audit |
| 8 | Continuous ATO surfaces an authorization-state derived only from canonical artifacts | Authorization-state derivation trace |

A deployment that satisfies criteria 1–4 but not 5–8 is *substrate-
conforming* but not *authorization-conforming*; this is a recognized
intermediate state during program execution and is explicitly anticipated
by the 90-Day Execution Plan.

---

## 8. Open Architecture Questions

Phase 1 closes the architecture baseline but explicitly leaves four
questions open for resolution by downstream phases. Each is recorded
here so the open set is visible to AOs and 3PAOs from the start.

| # | Question | Resolved By |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | The transport binding for each interface (event bus, API, pull) | Phase 2 — adapter-specific design |
| 2 | The exact signal-continuity mechanism at each boundary class | Boundary Modernization Appendix |
| 3 | The canonical evidence-pattern library per control family | Compliance & ATO Alignment |
| 4 | The phased sequencing of plane stand-up vs. boundary stand-up | 90-Day Execution Plan |

These questions are tracked as architectural debts, not deferrals — each
has a named downstream owner and a phase-gate dependency.

---

## 9. Hand-off to Downstream Phases

Phase 1 closes when this document is ratified. The downstream phases
consume it as follows:

- **Boundary Modernization Appendix** consumes §3 (Interfaces), §4
  (Signal Taxonomy), and §7 (Conformance) to specify the authorization-
  boundary redesign and the Signal Restoration Framework.
- **Compliance & ATO Alignment** consumes §5 (Control Binding Model)
  and §7 (Conformance) to produce the per-control crosswalk and the
  cATO operating model.
- **90-Day Execution Plan** consumes §2 (Plane Model) and §7
  (Conformance) to sequence plane stand-up against the conformance
  criteria.

No downstream commitment is made against an unratified Phase 1
architecture; this is the same sequencing rule established in Phase 0
§8 and is restated here for continuity.

---

::: {.callout-tip appearance="minimal"}
## Document Governance
This Phase 1 Architecture Document is a canonical program artifact.
Changes are governed by the canon-change protocol; substantive revisions
require an ADR, a schema check, and a drift scan. The document derives
from the Phase 0 Planning Document and is the upstream source for the
Boundary Modernization Appendix, Compliance & ATO Alignment, and the
90-Day Execution Plan.
:::
